stories: Who usually tells stories in your community?
What is a famous folk story from your area?
What lesson or moral does the story teach?
Who are the main characters in the story?
Where does the story take place (village, forest, temple, etc.)?
Are there songs, chants, or rhymes in the story?
What local words are commonly used in storytelling?
How do people start stories in your language?
How do people usually end stories?
Why is this story important to your community?
food: What is a popular traditional dish in your area?
What are the main ingredients used?
How is the dish prepared (cooking process)?
When is it usually eaten (daily, festivals, weddings)?
What cultural meaning does the dish hold?
Are there rituals connected to preparing this dish?
What spices make this food unique?
How is it served (special utensils, banana leaf, etc.)?
What is its local name in your language?
Why do people still prepare this dish today?
arts: What folk art is famous in your area (painting, weaving, dance)?
What materials are used to create it?
What symbols, patterns, or motifs are unique?
What is its religious or cultural importance?
How is this art taught to younger generations?
What challenges do artists face today?
Has the art form changed in modern times?
Why should this art be preserved?
festivals: What is the most important festival in your area?
What rituals or customs are followed?
What music, songs, or dances are performed?
How do people dress during the festival?
Who participates and what roles do they play?
What is the story or history behind the festival?
How has the festival changed over time?
Why do people value this festival?
music_dance: What is a traditional dance form in your area?
What music is used for it (instruments, songs)?
On what occasions is it performed?
What costumes or ornaments are worn?
What rhythms or steps are unique?
Are there folk songs tied to daily life?
How are these songs taught or remembered?
Why is it important to preserve them?
language_proverbs: Share a proverb or saying from your area.
What situation is it usually used in?
Who usually says it (elders, teachers, parents)?
Is it linked to a story or local belief?
Are there gestures/tone used with it?
What is an opposite proverb people say?
Has its meaning changed over time?
Why is it important in your culture?
games_sports: What games do children traditionally play?
What materials/equipment are used (stones, sticks, seeds)?
Are there seasonal games (monsoon, summer, harvest)?
Who usually plays (children, adults, men, women)?
Are these games linked to festivals?
What skills do these games teach (teamwork, strength)?
What is a unique game from your area?
What is its local name in your language?
Do people still play it today?
Why should these games be preserved?
